critique.summary: OpenCode is an open source AI coding agent that integrates with various coding environments and supports multiple AI models from different providers. It claims to be widely used, with over 120,000 GitHub stars, 800 contributors, and 5 million monthly users, emphasizing privacy by not storing user code or context data.
mainPoints: OpenCode is an open source AI coding agent available as a desktop app, terminal interface, and IDE extension.
It supports multiple AI models from providers like Claude, GPT, Gemini, and over 75 others via Models.dev.
Features include multi-session support, LSP integration, sharing session links, and login options for GitHub Copilot and ChatGPT Plus/Pro accounts.
The project claims significant community engagement with over 120,000 GitHub stars, 800 contributors, and 5 million monthly developers using it.
OpenCode prioritizes privacy by not storing users' code or context data.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about OpenCode being an open source AI coding agent with multi-platform support and integration with various AI models are consistent with typical features of similar tools. The GitHub statistics (120,000 stars, 800 contributors) and user base (5 million monthly developers) are plausible but should be verified directly on the project's GitHub repository and usage analytics. The privacy claim aligns with open source principles but requires confirmation from the project's privacy policy or independent audits. Overall, the article's claims are generally credible but lack external citations or multiple perspectives to fully verify the scale of usage and privacy assurances.
considerations: The article does not provide external sources or third-party reviews to validate the usage statistics or privacy claims.
It does not mention potential limitations or challenges users might face with OpenCode, such as model performance variability or compatibility issues.
There is no discussion of how OpenCode compares to other AI coding agents in terms of features, accuracy, or user experience.
The article lacks detailed information on security measures beyond the privacy claim.
No information is provided about the governance or funding of the OpenCode project, which could impact its sustainability.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on OpenCode, an open source AI coding agent, with users sharing mixed experiences regarding its development pace, feature set, and usability. While some praise its flexibility, model switching, and innovative features like MCP, others criticize its complexity, bugs, and resource inefficiency compared to alternatives like Claude Code.

comments.author: logicprog
comments.text:
OpenCode was the first open source agent I used, and my main workhorse after experimenting briefly with Claude Code and realizing the potential of agentic coding. Due to that, and because it's a popular an open source alternative, I want to be able to recommend it and be enthusiastic about it. The problem for me is that the development practices of the people that are working on it are suboptimal at best; they're constantly releasing at an extremely high cadence, where they don't even spend the time to test or fix things (or even build a proper list of changes for each release), and they add, remove, refine, change, fix, and break features constantly at that accelerated pace.

More than that, it's an extremely large and complex TypeScript code base — probably larger and more complex than it needs to be — and (partly as a result) it's fairly resource inefficient (often uses 1GB of RAM or more. For a TUI).

On top of that, at least I personally find the TUI to be overbearing and a little bit buggy, and the agent to be so full of features that I don't really need — also mildly buggy — that it sort of becomes hard to use and remember how everything is supposed to work and interact.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, balanced critique based on firsthand experience, highlighting both strengths and weaknesses, making it highly credible and insightful.

comments.author: everlier
comments.text:
OpenCode is an awesome tool.

Many folks from other tools are only getting exposed to the same functionality they got used to, but it offers much more than other harnesses, especially for remote coding.

You can start a service via `opencode serve`, it can be accessed from anywhere and has great experience on mobile except a few bugs. It's a really good way to work with your agents remotely, goes really well with TailScale.

The WebUI that they have can connect to multiple OpenCode backends at once, so you may use multiple VPS-es for various projects you have and control all of them from a single place.

Lastly, there's a desktop app, but TBH I find it redundant when WebUI has everything needed.

Make no mistakes though, it's not a perfect tool, my gripes with it:

- There are random bugs with loading/restoring state of the session

- Model/Provider selection switch across sessions/projects is often annoying

- I had a bug making Sonnet/Opus unusable from mobile phone because phone's clock was 150ms ahead of laptop's (ID generation)

- Sometimes agent get randomly stuck. It especially sucks for long/nested sessions

- WebUI on laptop just completely forgot all the projects at one day

- `opencode serve` doesn't pick up new skills automatically, it needs to be restarted
comments.analysis: The comment offers a nuanced overview of OpenCode's capabilities and limitations, reflecting practical usage and acknowledging imperfections.

comments.author: aimarketintel
comments.text:
One thing that makes coding agents really useful is structured data access via MCP servers. Instead of the agent trying to scrape a webpage to understand your project's context, you give it a direct API to query structured data from 9+ sources (GitHub repos, Stack Overflow questions, arXiv papers, npm packages).

The biggest bottleneck I've seen isn't the coding — it's the agent not having enough context about the ecosystem it's working in.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a technical feature (MCP) that enhances agent functionality, providing a thoughtful perspective on improving coding agents.

comments.author: brendanmc6
comments.text:
I’ve been extraordinarily productive with this, their $10 Go plan, and a rigorous spec-driven workflow. Haven’t touched Claude in 2 months.

I sprinkle in some billed API usage to power my task-planner and reviewer subagents (both use GPT 5.4 now).

The ability to switch models is very useful and a great learning experience. GLM, Kimi and their free models surprised me. Not the best, not perfect, but still very productive. I would be a wary shareholder if I owned a stake in the frontier labs… that moat seems to be shrinking fast.
comments.analysis: A practical user report emphasizing productivity and model flexibility, offering a credible perspective on competitive dynamics.

comments.author: JSR_FDED
comments.text: I’ve been having a very good experience with OpenCode and Kimi 2.5. It’s fast enough and smart enough that I can stay in a state of flow.
comments.analysis: A positive user testimonial that, while brief, reflects a genuine and favorable experience.

comments.author: softwaredoug
comments.text: The team also is not breathlessly talking about how coding is dead. They have pretty sane takes on AI coding including trying to help people who care about code quality.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a measured view of the development team's philosophy, adding context to the project's approach.

comments.author: solenoid0937
comments.text: The maintaining team is incredibly petty though. Tantrums when they weren't allowed to abuse Claude subscriptions and had to use the API instead. They just removed API support entirely.
comments.analysis: While somewhat emotional, this comment raises concerns about the team's professionalism and decision-making, which may affect user trust.

comments.text:
Stupid question, but are there models worth using that specialize in a particular programming language? For instance, I'd love to be able to run a local model on my GPU that is specific to C/C++ or Python. If such a thing exists, is it worth it vs one of the cloud-based frontier models?

I'm guessing that a model which only covers a single language might be more compact and efficient vs a model trained across many languages and non-programming data.
comments.analysis: A relevant and thoughtful question about model specialization and efficiency, reflecting common user considerations.

comments.author: hmcdona1
comments.text:
Can someone explain how Claude Code can instantly determine what file I have open and what lines I have selected in VS Code even if it's just running in a VS Code terminal instance, yet I cannot for the life of me get OpenCode to come anywhere close to that same experience?

The OpenCode docs suggest its possible, but it only works with their extension (not in an already open VS Code terminal) with a very specific keyboard shortcut and only barely at that.
comments.analysis: This comment points out a usability gap in OpenCode compared to competitors, highlighting integration challenges.

comments.author: sankalpnarula
comments.text: I personally like this better than claude code
comments.analysis: A brief personal preference statement without elaboration, less informative but reflects user sentiment.
